author: @daphneraven6745
text:
This is really awesome! Thank you very much for this Really practical tutorial that could get a person out of a jam at no expense.  

Most people have metal cans in the recycling or those pots and pans around the house. 
     Pretty much everybody has a can opener that could be used to punch holes. A Dremel with a cutting disc or a drillbit can do a lot of that work, or a person could just put water in something that has to be drilled and freeze it, and then use a nail to put the hole in the appropriate place in a pinch. Once holes are made, a person can use tinsnips or any other number of items to make the holes into the right size and shape necessary.

Even if supply is an issue at the time, a person could use some silica gel kitty litter to make thermal silicate insulation, and if they’re really clever, figure out how to use it to create a seal.

text:
Excellent project again!! This is one of my favourite tbh. Seen many versions of this using tin cans n al sorts bit this is a nice decent size!
Because these burn much more cleanly do you think this would be safe to use in doors with an out of use chimney that's been unboxed in.. eg. Still have a vent in outside the all never blocked up just boxed in fireplace. Used to have a back boiler.. 
If I were to remove the "boxed in" part BASICALLY just some plaster board, would this be fairly safe to use in a pinch over winter with the fuel shortages and blackouts planned? (As I've a few pallets I can use and a ton of brambles and thick bush like weeds to cut down that will be fairly dry soon!) 
Cheers from London 👍😎🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿🙏

author: @wthigo77
text: What I learned about rocket stoves years ago is that the chimney needs to be insulated or in a chamber that the heat runs through. (Rocket mass heater) it is the high temp of the chimney that makes it burn better and the holes you put at the bottom of the pipe are defeating that purpose. It has the name rocket stove from the sound it makes sucking so much air through the stove which kind sounds like a rocket. It takes a few minutes to heat up and properly work. This is a neat stove you made and I like it but is not a rocket stove. The term rocket stove is given to things that just resemble rocket stoves these days.

author: @ernestaircooling
text:
Spiral ducting is a very cheap easy material to make all kind of things, lots of sizes and thickness gauges !
A 90 degree bend pushed into a cut length and a steel grill pushed into it, 2 x Spiral Wall Mount Saddle Clamps for legs, drill a few holes and done !
this made in under 5 minutes for someone new to working with metals, only drilling a few holes and screwing in a few screws.
Even i can do it  !
Thanks again for a great video !

author: @gameoverwehaveeverypixelco1258
text:
Setup a few metres safely from home near a window, Wrap some copper pipe around the chimney, one of the copper pipes open to air, other end pointed inside home thru window to pull hot air inside for heating without fumes.
You could also have some pipes above chimney connected to a water tank for free hot water.
Add a oil drip to make fire last longer as a oil burner.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @PatGilliland
text: A Rishi stove perhaps? I am sure you have said so in the past, but it is incredibly important that any fuel burning stove used inside is in a properly ventilated location. Our houses in Canada are perhaps a bit more tightly sealed than most, but sadly almost every year people die from using a charcoal or similar stove for heat, not realizing how quickly carbon monoxide can build up to lethal concentrations.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate but said it - loads of times

replies.author: @PatGilliland
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering No worries mate, it bears repeating. I was talking around the real problem here in Canada, and that's with new immigrants from warmer climes not used to the cold climate and the level of weather sealing of the houses. It gets chilly, they pop on the same charcoal brazier they used back home for a little heat with the windows closed, and the result is an entire family very ill or possibly dead.These are not stupid people by any means but they are ignorant of the dangers and the need to take precautions.

With the cost of heating crisis in the UK, I can see a lot of people looking there for a cheap way to heat a room, and not having the same knowledge as their coal using grandparents (and the old coal fire flues long since bricked up) winding up very ill or dead.  Again not stupid - just ignorant.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Great stuff. 
Not sure if anyone else has attempted following 
Wondering how well a rocket stove would work made out of two concentric pieces of pipe, and a flat plate at bottom. Inner pipe being fair bit longer to generate the draft, and notched so air can flow down the outer pipe getting heated from the inner pipe, and the rise up the inner pipe. some rods and tubes to support inner tube, and also allowing for extra air to complete combustion  Drop lit fuel down the inner  pipe to get draft started... And once that hot, the incoming air will start to get heated.... Maybe high enough to start gassifying solid fuel dropped down the outer pipe.

author: @aronbraswell1589
text: another idea for this sort of build if you don't like the holes at the base of the chimney. add a piece of tubing to the top of the inside of the burn area with a L bend going up the chimney and its other end just outside the burner. The fire will super heat the air in the tube and that air will be pulled through by the draft to mix with the unburnt gasses. centering the tube end in the chimney works best.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - cheers

replies.author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
replies.text:
We have been trained to throw everything away! That's how capitalism literally capitalize on when things break and the basic principles of planned obsolescence, making you throw away still useful stuff.

Just think back to our grandparents and great-grandparents times, they hardly threw away anything. A broken Table might be tomorrows Chair, a Bowl and some Spoons.
Bones from the Sunday Roast? Hell, Glue has to be made somehow. A broken Knife or Tool? A Blacksmith will either repair it or trade it for a few coins and a new one.
So on and so forth....
Although I admit that my divorced was quite firm on her stand that its just me rationalizing my hoarding 🤨

author: @docink6175
text: there are several ways to make alcohol safer to use, you can fill the can with tissue paper, cotton balls, sand or diatomaceous earth. TP, sand and cotton absorb the alcohol and acts like a wick , DE becomes gel like, all can be reused but if you let the tp or cotton burn dry it will char. It can still be used charred.. This is a really sweet stove build.

author: @jamesross1003
text: Love a rocket stove.  Great build with just easy to find stuff.  I would like to see how many burns you get out of it.  Keep us updated on this one.  Thanks Rob!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: just a scammer mate - I have banned them and removed all of the comments - what a pain!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am thinking of changing this into a rocket kettle!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Oh nice!  Talk about hot water fast.  That might turn into a rocket boiler on you.   LOL.  Thanks for the reply.  I figured it was a scammer.  That's why I asked.

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Oh by the way if you do use it much, be aware of hexavalent chromium gas.  You probably already know that though.  I just want you to take care of yourself.  You have certainly inspired me to get back into some of those older projects that I have shelved. Thanks again!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: Chances are only your welds will give it off, so little danger I would think just during usage.  But you never know for sure the stainless quality.

author: @davec3990
text: Great video!  I'd like to make one.  Are there proper cross sectional area proportions of burn chamber to riser to secondary air?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - but basically most things will work - you can go into the math if you want but you can also cobble together something and be pretty sure it will work by rule of thumb

author: @mikebond6328
text: It gets so cold here I can’t get oil to burn on its own. It gets thicker than honey. Even propane is hard to burn when it gets that cold. I was thinking on making a hybrid wood and oil heater that I could get going with wood and then burn the waste oil or anything really.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a good idea mate use the wood fire as a preheater? maybe pass a pipe through the fire with the oil in the pipe - have to be quite a large bore pipe I would think if the oil is that viscous

author: @stonedsavage7814
text: Do you think there is a way to adapt it to biopellets? Would be interesting to see you try and make a woodpellet maker to recycle wood waste this was a very good video mate as usual 👍

replies.author: @jb-dn3ct
replies.text: You could fashion something of a tool to do this, I mean I have a 10 ton press but you could easily make one from a bottle jack and some thick steel welded into a frame, the end of the piston would be fine as a dye, all you'd need then is something to press sawdust into, I mean steel plates welded together with a hole drilled through fractionally bigger the outside diameter of the piston would be good enough, fill the hole with sawdust and compress it but yeah fair play I like your thinking

replies.author: @stonedsavage7814
replies.text: @jb-dn3ct thankyou and how would you suggest a way to have a Controlled feed? Gravity should do the trick but you lose heat control and a ton of efficiency too maybe changing the diameter of the feed pipe or maybe use a reduction fitting

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sure there is - I will have a think

replies.author: @jb-dn3ct
replies.text: @stonedsavage7814 I think this is going to make an interesting project and yes making the pellets is the easy part, I'd imagine the burn chamber would need to be modified as well, regarding timing of the pellet drop what would be cool is trying to scavenge the waste heat to do that job, something like a Stirling engine rotating an auger, or slightly more crazy, a turbo charger fitted to the exhaust with the impeller side modified to spin the auger, the wastegate could then be used to control the speed of rotation, I'm sure Robert will come up with something far simpler but this would be a cool video, hopefully watch this space 👍

replies.author: @stonedsavage7814
replies.text: @jb-dn3ct the turbocharger part is very interesting just need to degrease the bearings to make them low friction and then it could be powered by my misses backside emissions it will be that smooth at turning 😂😂

author: @williambianchi2006
text:
Paper logs work well in a rocket stove, as well. Free fuel advertisers send to your door. 😃
I've been wracking my brain for a way to use a small rocket stove like that to heat a room in my house. A heat exchanger blowing heat inside from the stove outside was one idea. Heating a metal pale of sand on the stove, then bringing it inside to radiate the stored heat is another.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good ideas mate

author: @matteonicoli
text:
I’m so happy with this subject.  Mr. Murray, the holy grail will be:
1. creating a small r heater
2. with the exhaust at the bottom, fits in regular chimney place, old appeasements
3. some sort of heat store
4. a batch or auto feeder (may be pallets)
5. save, can’t burn the place down
Hopefully 🙏 we can think of such a device together, because that would help a lot of people.  In any case 💓 many thanks, for your channel and style!

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: I've watched many videos on rocketstoves, rocket Mass heaters especially but this is the first time I've seen holes at the base of the riser?  Seems like a great idea.  Where did you come up with that?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it just makes sense to add air where the wood gas is - I was thinking about the fire triangle and trying to come up with a design that incorporated the basic principles of rocket stove design in a way that was easy to illustrate

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering very interesting!  I like it and I think it may solve a lot of issues if it works long term.  Now I'll have to experiment with it.  Biggest issue with these as you know, high temp always deteriorates them fast, even stainless.  I'm going to try 2-1 perlite to high temp cement.  Vermiculite is a better and amazing insulator but can not handle any moisture at all.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @l0I0I0I0 keep me in touch with how you get on will you? I am going to start that members forum next week I think

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering look forward to it!

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @l0I0I0I0 perhaps try using furnace cement. I use it to line my forge with. It'll easily handle your stove temps. It's economical, premixed to the optimum ratio and available at most hardware stores. If you want to be real fancy you could line the tins with kaowool first, but not entirely necessary for this application.

author: @intelligentgluteus-maximus4720
text: Hypothetically speaking, is this something that could be started by a window/some sort of ventilation inside and then once it comes up to temp where it is burning the wood gas, then close up the ventilation?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you shouldn't close up ventilation with any open flame fire mate - when you burn something you are using up oxygen all rooms with an open flame, gas, coal, wood - whatever need to have air coming in - but we are not talking opening the windows in a gale here - but they do need tone ventilated

If you research fire proof cements recipes you could get a stove that will last for years

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Home fire insurance in the UK must be high we have to have certified wood stove here in Canada or they cancel your insurance or the Fire Marshall demands it be removed. It would work great outside to heat water which can be piped in for heat.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: remember our housing stock can be hundreds of years old - if you instal new you have to have permission if you already have a fireplace no permission is needed and lots of houses here already have fireplaces in situ - usually for a couple of hundred years!

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text: I’d rather risk burning my house down (safely) than freeze to death because the gas has been cut.   I’ll deal with the insurance man when that issue presents it’s self.

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: It appears the threat of freezing to death is greater motivator than the actual fact. I burnt wood many years I just hope people understand the risk.

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: @joejoejoejoejoejoe4391 There are lots of solutions I always used an external air source when burning indoors so it now pressurizes the house to keep heat in.

replies.author: @Route72Trucks

author: @William_Hada
text: Very creative. I love it!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @smartliketruck
text:
Looks like to me that those loaf pans are PFTE (teflon) coated, keep well away from the smoke as it burns off.
Robert - I'd love it if you'd come up with a homebrew refractory material for making rocket stoves. Rocket stoves made with steel either don't come up to temperature for an efficient burn, or if they do start coming up to temperature the steel degrades quickly.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good point

replies.author: @smartliketruck
replies.text: @TheKlink  That involves building an aircrete foam setup. I'm not optimistic about the temperatures it could withstand but have yet to try it myself. I'm currently toying with phosphate based refractory vs. lye based

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: I've seen mixtures of peralite and cement 2-1 ratio for strength.  Not sure how well refractory cement would work but it would be worth a go, and should do fine and last a long time.  I would not mix metal into the mix as it tends to separate during temp changes due to different expansion ratios with the cement, UNLESS you are willing to find a material that has similar and close expansion ratios with each other AND at different temps.

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text:
NOBOX7. Done a refractory cement mixing quick lime and water glass which seemed impressive 
I’ve been thinking about this sort of stuff as building out of these types of materials seems more low tech 
I would think hydrated lime would work as it would turn back to oxide once it gets hot and it’s a lot more available 
A pottery mate of mine told me that sawdust is mixed with refractory cement  it burns out leaving voids filled with carbon a good insulator although that might burn out unless soaked in water glass first which might graphitise the carbon and that might make it heat conductive 
Im thinking of getting some porcelain clay as I want to get really high temperatures 

Lots to ponder 🧐
